pearl: Monkeypox has circulated endemically in West / Central Africa for decades. However, the current outbreak is unique as it has involved cases without links to endemic regions or zoonotic exposures, and case clusters have mapped onto sexual networks.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Documents historic endemic circulation in West/Central Africa since the 1970s.
Outbreak epidemiology showing 98% cases in MSM, mapping onto sexual networks.

pearl: Transmission of monkeypox primarily occurs through sustained skin-to-skin contact. Airborne / droplet transmission is possible but much less common, and requires an extended period of exposure in an enclosed, poorly ventilated space.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Describes person-to-person spread as inefficient and mainly via sustained skin-to-skin contact.
Supports fomite transmission via contaminated materials.
Related to transmission through contaminated items.
Supports fomite transmission possibility.

pearl: The clinical presentation of monkeypox observed in the current outbreak differs from the “textbook” historic presentation of monkeypox in several ways, including a reduced incubation period, less frequent prodromal symptoms, a higher likelihood of singular or several lesions rather than a generalized rash, and a notable prevalence of isolated proctitis or pharyngitis.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Details clinical presentation differences in the current outbreak vs textbook.

pearl: Testing for monkeypox involves collecting swabs from lesions and sending for viral DNA PCR.  For rectal swabs, self-collection by the patient is a good option.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: CDC guidance on specimen collection and PCR testing.
Study on self-collected vs provider-collected rectal swabs.

pearl: Supportive care is central to the management of monkeypox, including proper hydration, skin protectants, and NSAIDs and antihistamines. Opiates and IV NSAIDs can be used for severe pain; steroids should be avoided.
segment: Management of monkeypox: supportive care and antivirals
clinical_topic: Management of monkeypox: supportive care and antivirals
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: CDC supportive care measures including hydration, NSAIDs, antihistamines, pain control.
WHO supportive care guidance including perianal lesion measures.

pearl: There are three available antiviral agents for monkeypox: tecovirimat, brincidofovir, and cidofovir. All of these agents have yet to be studied for monkeypox in randomized controlled trials in humans. Brincidofovir and cidofovir should only be used with expert infectious disease consultation, and are notably teratogenic.
segment: Management of monkeypox: supportive care and antivirals
clinical_topic: Management of monkeypox: supportive care and antivirals
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: Anecdotal tecovirimat evidence and brincidofovir liver test elevation; no RCT.
Preclinical antiviral efficacy, underscoring lack of human RCTs.
Preclinical antiviral efficacy, contrasting absence of human RCTs.
Preclinical antiviral efficacy underscoring no RCTs.

pearl: There is a strong association between HIV and monkeypox infection in the current outbreak.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: 41% of US cases in people with HIV, framing strong association.
Outbreak epidemiology with STI overlap in MSM.
Higher hospitalization in HIV patients relevant to HIV-monkeypox association.

pearl: There are two available vaccines for monkeypox, both based on the vaccinia virus. The first is a live, attenuated, non-replicating form of vaccinia given in two doses 28 days apart. The second is a live, replication-competent form of vaccinia, and is administered via “scarification”; importantly, the live vaccine should not be given to immunocompromised patients.
episode_title: #359 Monkeypox with Dr. Boghuma Titanji

evidence_links.rationale: RCT showing similar immunogenicity of the two vaccines.
Two vaccines, similar immunogenicity, live vaccine contraindicated in immunocompromised.
Live vaccine risk in eczema patients supports contraindication.
Live vaccinia vaccine linked to myocarditis, a safety consideration.

pearl: Do not wait for permission to order steroids for cord compression.
episode_title: #358 Oncologic Emergencies with Dr. Aditi Singh

pearl: The most common presenting symptom of cord compression is back pain, and it may not present with the classic triad of cauda equina syndrome.
episode_title: #358 Oncologic Emergencies with Dr. Aditi Singh

evidence_links.rationale: Lawton 2019 states back pain is the most common presenting symptom of malignant cord compression.
Higdon 2018 supports suspicion for cord compression with vague back pain in cancer patients.

pearl: Malignant SVC syndrome can be associated with thrombus, which requires treatment with anticoagulation.
episode_title: #358 Oncologic Emergencies with Dr. Aditi Singh

evidence_links.rationale: Seligson 2022 covers SVC syndrome treatment including thrombolysis when thrombus is present.

pearl: Malignant bowel obstruction or pseudo-obstruction may be due to other risk factors besides current cancer diagnosis such as pain medications (e.g. opioids), prior surgeries, prior pelvic radiation, or current cancer-specific treatment.

pearl: Colorectal polyps can be categorized as neoplastic vs non-neoplastic.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

pearl: Hyperplastic polyps are the most common type of non-neoplastic polyp identified, and they do not have malignant potential.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

pearl: Neoplastic polyps include adenomas, sessile serrated polyps and rarely hamartomatous polyps.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

evidence_links.rationale: Erichsen 2016 supports importance of sessile serrated polyps as neoplastic lesions with cancer risk.

pearl: An advanced adenoma is an adenoma with any of the following three: size ≥10mm, tubulovillous or villous histology, high-grade dysplasia.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

pearl: Potential complications following polypectomy include bleeding, post polypectomy syndrome and bowel perforation.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

evidence_links.rationale: ASGE 2011 quantifies post-polypectomy bleeding risk.
Lohsiriwat 2010 describes perforation risk after polypectomy.
Reumkens 2016 describes post-polypectomy syndrome.

pearl: Bleeding is the most common adverse event following colonoscopy with polypectomy.
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

evidence_links.rationale: ASGE 2011 quantifies bleeding as common complication of polypectomy.

pearl: Based on the size and type of polyps identified, repeat surveillance is recommended ranging from 1 to 10 years
episode_title: #353 Colon Polyps

evidence_links.rationale: Gupta 2020 gives surveillance intervals ranging 1-10 years by polyp size/type.
Gupta 2020 USMSTF surveillance recommendations after polypectomy.

pearl: Prior history of ankle injury can predispose to recurrent injury due to impaired proprioception and ligamentous laxity

episode_title: #351 Live!  Foot and Ankle Pain with Dr. Joan Ritter

pearl: Runners with Achilles injuries should be counseled to stop running until recovered (or at least avoid hills)

pearl: Steroid injections are typically avoided in Achilles tendon injury
episode_title: #351 Live!  Foot and Ankle Pain with Dr. Joan Ritter

episode_title: #351 Live!  Foot and Ankle Pain with Dr. Joan Ritter

pearl: Insidious midfoot pain that worsens with weight-bearing is suspicious for navicular or metatarsal stress fracture
episode_title: #351 Live!  Foot and Ankle Pain with Dr. Joan Ritter

evidence_links.rationale: Monteagudo addresses navicular fracture non-healing and imaging for midfoot stress fracture.

pearl: Morton’s neuroma occurs in the 2nd or 3rd interspace of the toes, and occurs in the setting of wearing shoes with a narrow toe-box
episode_title: #351 Live!  Foot and Ankle Pain with Dr. Joan Ritter

evidence_links.rationale: Bahtia and Thomson discuss Morton's neuroma including toe-box footwear.

pearl: Hypoxemia refers to low oxygen content in the blood, however, what’s more clinically relevant is hypoxia which suggests reduced oxygen delivery and consequently, inadequate oxygen at the tissue-level, which can result in end-organ dysfunction/damage.
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: Hoiland oxygen content equation explains oxygen delivery to tissues, the basis for distinguishing hypoxia from hypoxemia.

pearl: Pulse oximeters are imperfect tools – interpret the results with caution! Similarly, blood gasses are helpful and have their place… in the right clinical scenario. Not every patient needs an ABG or VBG if they are hypoxemic!
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: Nitzan explains pulse oximeter function and limitations, directly supporting cautious interpretation.
Sjoding shows pulse oximeters miss occult hypoxemia in Black patients, supporting the caution claim.

pearl: First question when dealing with hypoxemia: Sick or not sick? That will guide your approach. Next, use history, exam, labs/rads and POCUS to help further evaluate your patient.
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: Dietrich B-lines support using POCUS as part of the evaluation of hypoxemia.

pearl: … BUT, treat the patient first! There is no reason NOT to put the patient on supplemental oxygen while performing your evaluation. In fact, it can be therapeutic AND diagnostic!
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

pearl: Remember the SIX major causes for hypoxemia. Shunt, V/Q mismatch and diffusion limitation feature reductions in the A-a difference. All causes of hypoxemia will correct – at least to some degree – with supplemental oxygen, EXCEPT SHUNTING!
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

pearl: Supplemental oxygen is a powerful treatment modality with many delivery systems – be familiar with the options and indications!
episode_title: #350 Rapid Response Series:Acute Hypoxemia

evidence_links.rationale: FLORALI supports HFNC as a delivery system option in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.

ASCEND showed CV benefit with bleeding tradeoff in higher-risk diabetics, informing benefit in this age group.

episode_title: #347 USPSTF Update: Aspirin for Primary Prevention of CVD

ASPREE showed no CV benefit and increased bleeding in healthy elderly, basis for avoiding aspirin in older adults.

episode_title: #347 USPSTF Update: Aspirin for Primary Prevention of CVD

evidence_links.paper_title: Aspirin Use to Prevent Cardiovascular Disease: Preventive Medication

pearl: Multiple sclerosis (MS) typically presents in young adults with a mean age of onset of 20-30 years-old; it is uncommon in individuals less than 10-years-old and greater than 50-years-old.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

evidence_links.rationale: McGinley 2021 directly states mean onset 20-30 years and rarity outside 10-50.

pearl: Optic neuritis is a common presenting symptom of MS.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

evidence_links.rationale: Same citation notes optic neuritis as a common presenting symptom of MS.

pearl: Optic neuritis vision changes occur predominantly in one eye and are often painful.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

evidence_links.rationale: Olek 2021 describes optic neuritis as painful, usually one eye, with subacute vision change.

pearl: Symptom persistence is key to diagnosing MS; they should be consistent and last at least 24-48 hours.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

pearl: MRI with contrast is the gold standard for imaging MS.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

pearl: Current and former smokers have worse MS outcomes.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

evidence_links.rationale: Rodgers 2022 states current and former smokers have worse MS outcomes.

pearl: In the last thirty years, we have gone from zero approved medications for MS to 23 disease modifying therapies (DMT).
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

pearl: While DMT for therapies should be managed by a specialist, the PCP can help by managing some of the symptoms of MS.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

evidence_links.rationale: Olek 2021 supports PCP symptom management and neurology referral in MS.

pearl: Infection can cause a “pseudo flare.” Treat the infection and the symptoms will resolve.
episode_title: #344 Multiple Sclerosis with Dr. Annette Okai

pearl: Immunotherapy has a century-plus long history, and is based on the principle that the immune system can attack specific “bad” or “other” cells, including tumor cells.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Immunotherapy harnesses the body’s own immune system to target cancer cells. The side effect profile is different from traditional chemotherapy, founded in the inflammation that ensues when the immune system is turned on.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) turn off the pause button on the immune response, and keep T-cells (and the immune response against cancer cells) active.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: There are two overall types of checkpoint inhibitors used across a variety of cancer types, ones that target CTLA-4, and ones that target PD-1/PD-L1.  The most common of them are pembrolizumab and nivolumab (which target PD-1), and ipilimumab (which targets CTLA-4).
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: The adverse effects from checkpoint inhibitors are byproducts of inflammation, and have parallels in autoimmune disorders of a variety of organs. They can occur at any point after initiation with checkpoint inhibitors, even years after stopping.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

evidence_links.rationale: Haanen 2020 addresses recurrence of irAEs on restarting therapy.

pearl: Endocrine complications of checkpoint inhibitors include hypothyroidism (more common than hyperthyroidism), adrenal insufficiency, and new onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Rheumatologic complications include seronegative sicca syndrome, inflammatory arthritis (typically negative for RF, CCP, and HLA-B27), and myositis.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

evidence_links.rationale: Calabrese 2018 describes ICI inflammatory arthritis as seronegative resembling RA/spondyloarthritis.
Ramos-Casals 2019 details sicca syndrome related to but distinct from Sjogren's.
Allenback 2020 covers ICI-associated myositis with proximal weakness and elevated CK.
Marini 2021 discusses ICI-associated myositis/polymyositis.

pearl: Treating immune-related adverse events (irAEs) typically involves steroids (except for the endocrine complications, the treatment for which involves giving hormones to treat the underlying deficiency).
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Ensure your patients have baseline labs (TSH, FT4, AM cortisol, basic metabolic panel, glucose, liver function tests, and a complete blood count) before starting checkpoint inhibitors.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: When unsure, ask for help–first, from your oncology colleagues, but also from endocrinology and rheumatology, as your patient’s case demands.
episode_title: #342 Checkpoint Inhibitors:

pearl: Skip imaging when a patient has a normal neurological exam and has symptoms consistent with a migraine headache.  If history/exam is suggestive of a secondary headache, order an MRI of the brain without contrast for most patients.

Rimegepant as acute rescue agent.
Rimegepant every other day for prophylaxis.

Cited as oldest of four CGRP monoclonal antibodies.

Explains CGRP mechanism supporting gepant abortive/preventive use.

pearl: Gallstones are common, patients with asymptomatic gallstones do not need a cholecystectomy.
episode_title: #340 Gallbladder Disease

evidence_links.rationale: Sakorafas 2007 shows only ~25% of asymptomatic patients develop symptoms over 10 years, supporting no cholecystectomy for asymptomatic gallstones.

pearl: Typical presentation of biliary pain: moderate-severe pain, which may be diffuse abdominal/epigastric or localized to the right upper quadrant and may radiate to the back.  Typically pain episodes last from 30 minutes to several hours. Biliary pain classically should not include dyspepsia, bloating, or gas but is frequently associated with nausea.
episode_title: #340 Gallbladder Disease

evidence_links.rationale: Latenstein 2021 supports that dyspepsia-type symptoms are unlikely due to cholelithiasis, aligning with pearl's exclusion of dyspepsia from biliary pain.

pearl: Up to 40% of patients may have continued pain after a cholecystectomy, so it is important to confirm that the abdominal pain your patient is experiencing is truly from gallstones before surgery (and not blame their functional dyspepsia on incidentally noted gallstones).
episode_title: #340 Gallbladder Disease

evidence_links.rationale: van Dijk 2009 documents 40% of patients with post-cholecystectomy pain, directly supporting the claim.

pearl: Ultrasound is the imaging of choice for cholelithiasis.
episode_title: #340 Gallbladder Disease

evidence_links.rationale: Benarroch-Gampel 2011 supports ultrasound as first-line imaging for cholelithiasis.

pearl: Tapering long term stable, high dose opioids may increase the risk of overdose and mental health crisis.
episode_title: #336 Updates in Addiction Medicine: Live From SGIM

evidence_links.rationale: Retrospective study of 100,000+ patients linking tapering to higher overdose and mental health crisis.

pearl: Engage in shared-decision making with your patient (and regularly screen for opioid use disorder) when you first meet a patient on long-term opioids, before deciding whether and how to taper.  If you and your patient do decide to taper opioids, go slow.
episode_title: #336 Updates in Addiction Medicine: Live From SGIM

evidence_links.rationale: Study showed rapid tapers >10%/month linked to more complications, supporting going slow.

pearl: Continue buprenorphine perioperatively, per the latest society consensus guidelines. You can divide the dose over the day to achieve better pain control.
episode_title: #336 Updates in Addiction Medicine: Live From SGIM

evidence_links.rationale: Consensus guidelines recommending continuing buprenorphine perioperatively.
Retrospective data on dose holds and overdose supporting continuing/restarting buprenorphine perioperatively.

pearl: Not all patients with anxiety may fit the traditional paradigm. Be thoughtful during your assessment, especially when patients present with unexplained somatic complaints.

pearl: CGMs can be helpful in patients without diabetes, but only if clinicians and patients understand what counts as signal versus noise. Brief postprandial spikes can be physiologic; persistent elevation is more concerning.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: Guideline noting non-diabetic glucose rises into 160-180 range, distinguishing physiologic spikes.
Supports focus on duration of elevation vs peak in CGM interpretation.

pearl: A flat glucose trace is not the same thing as low cardiovascular risk. If a dietary pattern lowers glucose excursions but raises ApoB, that may represent a dangerous tradeoff.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

pearl: ApoB is often more useful than LDL-C when the standard lipid panel may be underestimating atherogenic risk, especially in patients with insulin resistance, elevated triglycerides, central adiposity, or metabolic syndrome.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

pearl: Lp(a) helps quantify inherited cardiovascular risk and may justify more aggressive treatment of modifiable risk factors even when the standard lipid panel looks reassuring.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: Lp(a) as marker of inherited CV risk justifying aggressive management of modifiable factors.

pearl: Muscle is a major glucose sink. Resistance training improves insulin sensitivity and should be part of nearly every metabolic health plan.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: Muscle improves insulin sensitivity and supports metabolic function.
Resistance training as cornerstone; muscle acts as glucose sink improving insulin sensitivity.

pearl: VO2 max and muscle mass are not vanity metrics. They help clinicians and patients work backwards from the functional goals of older age.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

pearl: Sleep is not a luxury variable. Chronic sleep deprivation can worsen blood pressure, triglycerides, glucose regulation, and overall metabolic health.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: Poor sleep associated with worsening BP, triglycerides, and glucose handling.
Sleep architecture and chronic stress state relate to sleep's metabolic importance.

pearl: Wearables can be useful teaching tools, but they can also drive “data neurosis,” “orthosomnia,” and unnecessary obsession with physiologic perfection.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: HRV as directional marker relates to interpreting wearable data thoughtfully.

pearl: The most effective interventions in preventive medicine are still the “core four”: sleep, nutrition, fitness, and emotional resilience.
episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: Aerobic activity guideline supports fitness as core intervention.

episode_title: #520: Healthspan Medicine, A Practical Approach

evidence_links.rationale: BPC-157 harm consideration in structured peptide risk framework.
Limited RCT evidence for BPC-157 supports cautious structured counseling.

pearl: There are two phases to covid 19: viral phase vs immune mediated phase
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

pearl: These two phases should guide the best approach to treatment. For example, if there is no evidence of an inflammatory phase, then the patient does not warrant immunomodulators.
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

evidence_links.rationale: RECOVERY showed steroids may be harmful without oxygen requirement, supporting withholding immunomodulators absent inflammatory phase.

pearl: Immunocompromised patients can present unique challenges with regards to diagnosis as they can present in a multitude of ways, often in vastly differents ways than immunocompetent patients. At times they can have high inflammatory response without the classic signs/symptoms.
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

pearl: Steroids are first line for immunomodulators whereas JAK and IL-6 inhibitors are reserved for patients who are worsening despite adequate steroid therapy.
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

evidence_links.rationale: RECOVERY dexamethasone establishes steroids as first-line immunomodulator.
Tocilizumab (IL-6) as add-on when worsening despite steroids.
Baricitinib (JAK) as add-on when worsening despite steroids.

pearl: The rate of bacterial coinfection is low, so reserve empiric antibiotics for when there is evidence of bacterial infection.
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

evidence_links.rationale: Langford meta-analysis shows low bacterial coinfection rate, arguing against empiric antibiotics.

pearl: Therapeutic anticoagulation is recommended for floor patients with hypoxemia and elevated D-dimer,but if a patient requires a higher level of care in ICU, the current recommendation is to step down to prophylactic dosing.
episode_title: #332 Inpatient Covid Management with Dr. Nathan Erdmann.

evidence_links.rationale: Goligher shows therapeutic anticoagulation benefits floor patients but not ICU patients.

pearl: Prolonged bowel rest is not recommended. Early enteral nutrition prevents bacterial translocation and reduces infection.

evidence_links.rationale: Boxhoorn supports enteral nutrition maintaining mucosal barrier and lowering infection risk.
Forsmark supports early enteral nutrition within 24 hours and avoiding TPN.
Goodchild supports early oral feeding and limiting TPN.

pearl: Fever and leukocytosis at an early stage doesn’t mandate empiric antibiotic use, as acute pancreatitis alone does not warrant prophylactic antibiotics. Antibiotics may be useful for alternative known infections (i.e. cholangitis, aspiration pneumonia, etc.)

evidence_links.rationale: Boxhoorn states antibiotics should be reserved for true infectious complications.

pearl: Hereditary hemochromatosis is most commonly due to two inherited genetic mutations in C282Y leading to loss of iron-regulated hepcidin production and reduced hepcidin levels.

evidence_links.rationale: Olynyk describes significance of C282Y homozygosity/compound heterozygosity for iron overload.

pearl: The genetic mutations associated with hereditary hemochromatosis are common but the penetrance is variable and much lower.
episode_title: #380 Hemochromatosis with Elliot Tapper

evidence_links.rationale: Cabrera cited for high carrier prevalence while penetrant disease is much less common.
Odufalu emphasizes mutations common but clinically significant disease in minority.

pearl: Iron indices that increase the likelihood of hereditary hemochromatosis include ferritin >200 in females >300 males and TSAT >45% females or >50% in males.
episode_title: #380 Hemochromatosis with Elliot Tapper

pearl: The differential diagnosis of an elevated ferritin and TSAT in an inpatient setting is broad and iron indices to evaluate for hemochromatosis should be checked outside of acute hospitalization..
episode_title: #380 Hemochromatosis with Elliot Tapper

evidence_links.rationale: Crook cited for differential of very high ferritin including malignancy, sepsis, Still's, HLH.
Fauter cited for causes of very high ferritin in inflammatory/infectious states.
Hearnshaw cited for high ferritin in alcohol-related and inflammatory states.
Senjo supports hemochromatosis is uncommon cause of elevated ferritin in inpatients.

pearl: Following a diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis, genetic testing is recommended for all first degree relatives.

pearl: Elevated venous pressure/venous congestion, rather than reduced cardiac index/poor forward flow, is thought to be the major contributor to the pathophysiology of cardiorenal syndrome.

evidence_links.rationale: Hanberg 2016 shows reduced cardiac index is not the primary driver; most decompensated HF patients have preserved cardiac indices.
Mullens 2009 supports venous back pressure over arterial hypoperfusion in ADHF.
Mullens 2020 supports elevated venous congestion as key in cardiorenal syndrome.
Damman 2009 supports venous back pressure and impaired perfusion in ADHF.
Rangaswami 2019 emphasizes venous congestion role in cardiorenal pathophysiology.

pearl: Home ACE-is/ARBs and SGLT-2 inhibitors do not necessarily need to be stopped in patients with acute decompensated heart failure with an increase in creatinine, as long as the patient is not hypotensive or hyperkalemic. If these medications are stopped during hospitalization, be sure to restart them prior to discharge.

pearl: A creatinine “bump” with diuresis does not necessarily signify kidney injury. Allow for “permissive hypercreatinemia” while diuresing. Avoid stopping diuresis prematurely with residual congestion on board simply because of a rise in creatinine.

evidence_links.rationale: Ahmad 2018 supports that creatinine rise during diuresis does not indicate kidney injury.
Brisco 2016 DOSE post-hoc: creatinine rise associated with improved outcomes.
DOSE analyses: AKI-criteria creatinine rises paradoxically associated with reduced morbidity/mortality.
ESCAPE: hemoconcentration paradoxically associated with reduced morbidity/mortality.
ROSE-AHF: creatinine changes not correlated with kidney injury biomarkers during decongestion.

pearl: Invasive measures like right heart catheterizations and non-invasive measures like POCUS can help clarify volume status.

pearl: Cardiac biomarkers such as troponin and BNP are often chronically elevated in patients with CKD, and correlate with worse prognosis. A relative change in BNP from baseline can be informative in patients in CKD.

Michos 2014: CKD with elevated biomarkers worse outcomes.
Wang 2020: elevated cardiac biomarkers in CKD associated with worse outcomes.

pearl: Given the difficulties with obtaining high-quality standardized blood pressure readings in the office, out-of-office home blood pressure measurement is recommended to confirm a diagnosis of hypertension. Patients should be instructed to acquire a validated blood pressure machine and educated on the steps of taking their blood pressure. A minimum of three days of home blood pressure readings (two back-to-back readings every AM and two every PM) is sufficient.
segment: How to make a diagnosis of hypertension
clinical_topic: How to make a diagnosis of hypertension
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Bello 2018 supports the 3-day AM/PM home BP schedule.
USPSTF Grade A recommendation for out-of-office confirmation of hypertension.
ISH guidance on proper office technique underlies difficulty of office measurement.
Drawz 2020 shows discordance between routine office and standardized readings.

pearl: For an initial blood pressure regimen, low-dose combination therapy is preferable to single agent therapy. A low-dose calcium channel blocker + ARB is a good starting regimen. Choose ARBs over ACE inhibitors due to their superior side effect profile. ARBs should be first-line in blood pressure management..
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Salaam 2019 supports low-dose combination therapy over monotherapy.
Chen 2021 supports preferring ARBs over ACEis for superior side-effect profile.
Brown 1996 shows higher cough/angioedema with ACEis supporting ARB preference.
Elliot 1996 evidence of higher cough/angioedema with ACEis favoring ARBs.
Miller 2008 evidence of higher ACEi adverse effects supporting ARB choice.
ALLHAT post-hoc analyses relevant to CCB/ARB starting choice discussion.

pearl: 24 hour ambulatory blood pressure measurements are useful to diagnose white coat hypertension.
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Cohen 2019 meta-analysis on white coat hypertension outcomes.
Dolan 2004 characterizes white coat hypertension epidemiology.

pearl: For patients with chronic kidney disease, fear of a creatinine bump should not discourage providers from using ACEis/ARBs; on the contrary, these medications are protective in patients with kidney disease. Up to a 30% rise in creatinine with initiation of an ACEi/ARB is acceptable. Close monitoring of potassium is important, with a potassium threshold of 5.5.
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: KDIGO guidelines state up to 30% creatinine rise acceptable with ACEi/ARB.
Jafar 2001 shows ACEi/ARB protective in CKD with proteinuria.
Mann 2001 shows cardiovascular benefit of RAAS blockade in CKD.

pearl: Diuretics are underutilized in patients with chronic kidney disease. Chlorthalidone is a promising agent in this population based on the CLICK trial.
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: CLICK trial supports chlorthalidone in advanced CKD.

pearl: In a patient with refractory hypertension, primary hyperaldosteronism should be on the differential. Testing serum renin and aldosterone does not require stopping your patient’s anti-hypertensive medications (with the exception of mineralocorticoid antagonists (MRA) and amiloride).
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Seifarth 2002 supports continuing antihypertensives during aldosterone screening.

pearl: Mineralocorticoid antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) are useful in treating patients with primary hyperaldosteronism.
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Monticone 2018 relates to amiloride/aldosterone-driven hypertension treatment.

pearl: Salt-sensitive hypertension is likely if renin is suppressed even when aldosterone is not elevated.  Thus, target renin suppression (ex: MRA or amiloride). Amiloride is a particularly good treatment option for this population.
episode_title: #321 Hypertension FAQ: Common Outpatient Cases with Dr. Jordy Cohen

evidence_links.rationale: Monticone 2018 supports amiloride for suppressed-renin/Liddle-like salt-sensitive hypertension.

pearl: The differential diagnosis for chronic pelvic pain is  long and many patients have multiple etiologies for their pain. Instead of looking for the one diagnosis, focus on the main organ systems: bladder, bowel, muscles/nerves, reproductive organs, and/or centrally mediated pain.

pearl: Viscero-visceral sensitization is the concept that organs in the pelvis share neuronal connections (a spider web of nerves instead of one nerve to each organ), so patients with chronic pelvic pain can experience symptoms across organ systems (ex: a patient with dysmenorrhea has urinary urgency and diarrhea with her menses).

pearl: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common extraintestinal feature of celiac disease (CD), and is commonly overlooked in menstruating women.
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: Grisolano cited for iron deficiency anemia as most common extraintestinal feature, often overlooked in menstruating women.

pearl: Not all patients with CD have diarrhea; 10-20%  of CD patients have constipation as the predominant bowel habit.
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: McElvaney supports constipation presentation in ~15% of CD patients.

pearl: CD is not a disease limited to children; nearly 20% of patients are 60 years or older at the time of initial CD diagnosis.
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: Cranney supports ~20% diagnosed at age 60 or older.

pearl: Serology test of choice to screen for CD is IgA TTG + total IgA level; only run a second serological test (e.g., deamidated gliadin peptide antibodies) if the clinical scenario or histology is not congruent with initial serology. Avoid panel testing!
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: Rubio-Tapia guideline recommends individual serologic tests over panels and discourages anti-gliadin testing.

pearl: Small bowel biopsies are indicated for anyone with a positive serology as well as those with negative serology but high clinical suspicion.
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: Lewis supports ~5% seronegative CD, justifying biopsy despite negative serology.

pearl: Testing for permissive haplotypes for CD (e.g., HLA-DQ2 and -DQ8) can be useful to rule out CD (high negative predictive value) and can be considered in cases where there is serologic and histologic discordance.
episode_title: #300 Celiac Disease with Dr. Amy Oxentenko

evidence_links.rationale: Karell supports HLA-DQ2/DQ8 high negative predictive value for ruling out CD.

pearl: Bronchiectasis definition: airway widening on CT imaging (larger airway than accompanying vessel); symptoms: usually include purulent sputum, dyspnea/exercise intolerance, cough and potentially malaise and/or hemoptysis.
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: Shafer 2018 provides the CT-based definition of bronchiectasis (airway wider than accompanying vessel).

pearl: Consider eval for bronchiectasis (CT chest) for patients treated for “non-resolving” pneumonia/COPD exacerbations, or, those with obstructive PFTs without a smoking history- especially those encountered in their 5th or 6th decade of life.
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: Akasmit 2017 supports disease burden especially in older adults, relevant to who to evaluate.

pearl: Sputum sampling is incredibly helpful to identify pathogenic organisms that, in the event of an exacerbation, can be targeted for treatment.
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: Park 2015 supports sending sputum for cultures including AFB to identify pathogens requiring different treatment.

pearl: In stable bronchiectasis, airway clearance (+/- hypertonic saline + active cycle breathing maneuvers + some form of vibrating positive pressure device) forms the cornerstone of therapy.
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: Lewis 2012 supports airway clearance with hypertonic saline, active breathing, and oscillatory device.
O'Neill 2018 supports the airway clearance regimen as cornerstone of therapy.
Wark 2018 shows hypertonic saline improves lung function in CF bronchiectasis, supporting airway clearance.

pearl: Bronchiectasis exacerbation definition: 48+ hours of worsening symptoms (3 or more of the hallmark symptoms) plus a clinician’s decision to change/add therapy based upon the clinical presentation.
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: Hill 2017 gives the granular definition: 3+ symptoms worsening 48h plus clinician decision to change therapy.
Pasteur 2010 provides the earlier ATS exacerbation definition framing exacerbation recognition.

pearl: Non-tuberculosis mycobacteria (NTM) are environmental pathogens that normally don’t cause disease in immunocompetent patients with normal lung architecture, common agents include Mycobacterium Avium Complex (MAC),
episode_title: #306 Bronchiectasis and NTM: You can’t get enough sputum!

evidence_links.rationale: McShane 2015 supports NTM as environmental organisms with diverse species.
2020 NTM guidelines discuss MAC as common agent and when pathogenic.

pearl: Focus on individualizing the care for each pregnant patient. Emphasize risk/benefit analysis for each medical condition and medication.
episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

pearl: Ask about the patient’s pregnancy history. A history of gestational diabetes increases the risk of developing type 2 diabetes later in life. History of preeclampsia increases the likelihood of future cardiovascular disease. It is important to know this so that the patient can be monitored accordingly.

episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

pearl: Most SSRIs are safe in pregnancy. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends against use of paroxetine, however.
episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

pearl: For pregnant patients with headache: extra strength tylenol, metoclopramide, caffeine, and hydration.
episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

pearl: For pregnant patients with nausea, doxylamine and pyridoxine, and can also consider metoclopramide or an anti-histamine.
episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

pearl: There is a risk of postpartum depression up to one year after delivery. PHQ-9 or Edinburgh Postnatal Depression can be used at each visit to monitor.
episode_title: #305 OB for the Internist: What to Expect

evidence_links.rationale: Howard 2014 supports postpartum depression being common after delivery.

topic: COVID-19 Vaccine Updates and Outpatient Treatment
pearl: Patients over 65 and those with immunosuppression should receive an extra COVID vaccine.  Younger patients with comorbidities or risk of exposure are at lower risk, but may choose to get a booster.

pearl: It is safe and effective to mix and match vaccines, ie two-dose Pfizer series and one Moderna booster (half dose or 50 mcg), or the one-dose J&J series and one Pfizer booster (full dose or 30 mcg).

Nordstrom shows finishing with mRNA dose improves immunity for J&J.

topic: Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist
pearl: Assessing acute pain:  Ask the patient where the pain is the most severe, to describe the characteristics (e.g. dull, achy), determine if it is acute vs. chronic and review current medical and/or surgical conditions.
episode_title: #299 Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

topic: Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

episode_title: #299 Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

topic: Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist
pearl: All patients should start with non-opioid analgesics such as acetaminophen, NSAIDS, and muscle relaxants for acute pain.
episode_title: #299 Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

topic: Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist
pearl: Selecting an opioid: One tip is to ask patients in a non-judgmental manner, “what has worked for you in the past in regards to pain management? Genetic polymorphisms exist in how individuals respond to different opioids, thus assessing a patient’s prior experience can help you select an opioid for their pain management.
episode_title: #299 Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

evidence_links.rationale: Pereira Vieira 2019 describes genetic polymorphisms leading to different analgesic responses across opioids.

episode_title: #299 Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist

topic: Acute Pain Management for the Hospitalist
pearl: Opioid Exit Strategy: When indicated for patients who have been on a high dose of opioids for several days, co-create an opioid taper plan with the patient and write it out explicitly. Be sure to partner with a patient’s outpatient provider and nursing facility physician to help ensure a safe taper.

pearl: Stable angina and unstable angina/ACS have inherently different pathophysiologies.  The plaque blockages in stable angina are hard and fixed, so the disease does not tend to change acutely.  In ACS, patients have sudden plaque rupture with coagulation, inflammation, and acute blood flow limitation leading to infarction.

pearl: The pillars of dementia prevention and treatment rest on maintaining an overall healthy lifestyle, good physical health/optimal comorbidity management, and adequate social support (notice we didn’t say medications…)
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

evidence_links.rationale: Horder shows midlife fitness associated with reduced dementia, supporting healthy lifestyle pillar.
Livingston supports healthy eating, sleep, social interaction, and activity in dementia care.
Vandepitte supports caregiver education/social support role in dementia care.

pearl: Standard assessments for dementia include the MOCA, MMSE, and SLUMS. While MOCA is sensitive for picking up mild deficits, it is a longer test and may be challenging for those with a higher symptom burden.
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

evidence_links.rationale: Oh cites MMSE, MOCA, SLUMS as validated cognitive assessments.

pearl: Removing the stigma and shame of dementia is important. The clinician should ameliorate this by respecting patients and offering ways to achieve their overall goals for a good quality of life.
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

evidence_links.rationale: Caregiver education supports quality of life/support goals but not stigma directly.

pearl: Keep things simple. If you are scratching your head about ordering complex tests or imaging for atypical cases (e.g., younger patients or people with rapidly progressive dementia), these patients should be referred.
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

pearl: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and memantine show marginal clinical improvement in trials and the number needed to harm for acetylcholinesterase inhibitors is 12.
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

evidence_links.rationale: Arvanitakis review reinforces limited efficacy of dementia pharmacotherapy.
Knight meta-analysis reports MMSE improvement of 1.0 not clinically significant.
Lanctot cited for AChEI harms and number needed to harm of 12.
Raina shows only marginal clinically insignificant improvement with AChEIs and memantine.
Parsons Cochrane review of stopping meds showed decline not clinically significant, reinforcing marginal benefit.

pearl: In rare cases, antipsychotics are needed to manage dangerous dementia-related behaviors (though this is not the first-line treatment!) If needed, use at the lowest dose possible for the shortest duration. The number needed to harm (where harm is an associated death) is 26-50.
episode_title: #268 Dementia Made Simple with Dr. Josh Uy

evidence_links.rationale: Maust cited for antipsychotic death risk with NNH 26-50.

pearl: Additional etiologies of non-osmotic diarrhea include 1) Celiac disease 2) Chronic infections 3) Inflammatory Bowel Disease 4) Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth 5) Bile Acid malabsorption 6) Fat malabsorption 7) Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency and 8) Disorders of the Gut-Brain Axis.

pearl: The SPEP identifies the presence of an abnormal immunoglobulin (M-protein), and its concentration.  It is used in conjunction with Immunofixation
episode_title: #247 SPEP It Up

pearl: to tell us the type of Immunoglobulin being overproduced–which type of heavy chain (IgA, IgM, or IgG) and which type of light chain (kappa or lambda).

pearl: Monoclonal gammopathies are disorders in which too much of one particular Immunoglobulin (or M-Protein) is produced, resulting in an M-Spike on SPEP (most of the time).  Monoclonal gammopathies are otherwise known as paraproteinemias.
episode_title: #247 SPEP It Up

pearl: Refer anyone with an abnormal SPEP to Hematology.

pearl: Hepatic steatosis is pathologic fatty infiltration of the liver. The two most common etiologies of hepatic steatosis are 1) non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and 2) alcohol .
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

pearl: NAFLD is hepatic steatosis associated with metabolic syndrome, and it includes NAFL, NASH and NASH cirrhosis.
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

pearl: Fatty liver disease is extremely common (~25% global population), and beware that ~20% may progress to the more dangerous NASH, indicating inflammation.
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

evidence_links.rationale: Younossi 2016 cited for the ~25% global prevalence of NAFLD.
Sheka 2020 states about 20% of NAFLD patients develop NASH.

pearl: A significant number of patients with NASH will develop hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

evidence_links.rationale: Sheka 2020 notes 20-40% of NASH patients develop fibrosis/cirrhosis.

pearl: Despite what your laboratory reference value is, ALT >25 IU/L (female) or >33 IU/L (male) is abnormal and associated with liver morbidity.
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

evidence_links.rationale: Kwo 2016 provides sex-specific ALT reference ranges matching the pearl's thresholds.

pearl: The initial laboratory workup for NAFLD should include: hepatitis C antibody, hepatitis B core and surface antigen, alpha-1 antitrypsin, and ferritin +/- transferrin saturation.
episode_title: #227 Baffled by NAFLD?

evidence_links.rationale: Chalasani/AASLD guideline cited for initial lab evaluation and ruling out other liver disease causes.

pearl: A history of orthopnea and a physical exam showing increased JVP are the cornerstones of diagnosing ADHF. Crackles are not specific (Dr Kittleson doesn’t listen to the lungs!)
episode_title: #230 Kittleson Rules Acute Heart Failure

evidence_links.rationale: Wang et al 2005 directly establishes orthopnea specificity, elevated JVP as specific findings, and that crackles are neither sensitive nor specific for ADHF.
Drazner 2001 supports specificity of physical exam findings (S3) for ADHF, complementing the exam-based diagnosis point.

pearl: UTIs are common. Diagnosis is not easy or straightforward. When choosing an antibiotic, avoid collateral damage and keep it brief.
episode_title: #231 U Talkin’ UTIs Re: Me?

evidence_links.citation_label: Cortes-Penfield, Infect Dis Clin North Am 2017
evidence_links.rationale: Cortes-Penfield supports difficulty of UTI diagnosis via urinalysis interpretation.

pearl: First line agents in the US are nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX or fosfomycin.
episode_title: #231 U Talkin’ UTIs Re: Me?

evidence_links.rationale: IDSA guideline lists first-line agents including nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, fosfomycin, and notes fosfomycin failure and pivmecillinam unavailability in US.

pearl: Infrequent recurrences can be treated with first line agents. Relapses (early return of symptoms) or multiple recurrences warrant a culture to rule out MDRO.
episode_title: #231 U Talkin’ UTIs Re: Me?

pearl: Not all UTIs in men are complicated! First line agents can be used.
episode_title: #231 U Talkin’ UTIs Re: Me?

evidence_links.rationale: VA study on men with UTI found no benefit to prolonged treatment, supporting standard treatment approaches in men.

pearl: Drugs for the lower urinary tract (bladder and below): Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin.
episode_title: #231 U Talkin’ UTIs Re: Me?

pearl: Serum sodium is used as a marker for serum tonicity. While serum sodium and osmolality generally agree, this is not always the case.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Adrogué and Madias referenced for sodium and tonicity not always agreeing.
Rondon-Berrios cited to illustrate low sodium with high tonicity coexisting.

pearl: Low sodium with normal osmolality is likely a lab error- high protein can interfere with sodium measurement. The cause of high protein should be investigated (ex: IVIG, lipid panel, or serum protein electrophoresis). This is pseudohyponatremia.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: Low sodium with high osmolality is due to high levels of an additional osmolite, most likely glucose, drawing water into the blood.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Hillier and Barrett for correcting sodium in hyperglycemia.

pearl: Hypoosmolar-hyponatremia is most common, which means that the low sodium and osmolarity are concordant- this can be ADH dependent (CHF, SIADH, etc) or independent (tea-and-toast, primary polydipsia, oliguric kidney failure) .
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: Tea-and-toast or beer potomania hyponatremia occur when low solute intake limits the ability of the kidneys to produce urine that maintains electrolyte homeostasis.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: SSRIs, anti-epileptics, sulfonylureas, and opioids can lead to SIADH mediated hyponatremia.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

evidence_links.rationale: Shepshelovich directly supports SSRIs, antiseizure, sulfonylureas, opioids causing SIADH.
Liamis cited as evidence medications can cause SIADH-mediated hyponatremia.

pearl: Normal saline can improve hyponatremia due to hypovolemia, but it worsens hypervolemic-hyponatremia (ex: heart failure) and can lower the sodium in SIADH.
episode_title: REBOOT #48 Hyponatremia Deconstructed

pearl: Subacute/chronic cough is one of the most common primary care complaints seen by providers
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

pearl: The most common cause for acute (and subacute cough) is active or recent viral infection (“post-viral cough syndrome”) for which a trial of inhaled corticosteroid is reasonable
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

evidence_links.rationale: Braman 2006 explains post-viral cough mechanisms (airway hypersensitivity, post-nasal drip).
Irwin 2017 links viral infection to airway hyperreactivity in acute cough and defines cough durations.

pearl: Other common causes for cough include gastroesophageal reflux disease, post-nasal drip / upper-airway cough syndrome, cough-variant asthma and non-asthmatic eosinophilic bronchitis
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

evidence_links.rationale: Côté 2020 covers cough-variant asthma and non-asthmatic eosinophilic bronchitis with ICS treatment.
Smith 2010 supports esophageal reflux as a cause of cough.
Smith 2016 supports reflux as cause of cough warranting treatment.

pearl: The first step in evaluating cough should not be labs and imaging, but rather a thorough history focusing on things like onset, aggravating/alleviating factors, quality of the cough and exposure history
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

pearl: Chest X-ray can be useful to identify major anatomic abnormalities, CT can be used later on if cough persists to rule out more subtle disease states
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

pearl: Basic spirometry can be helpful to identify obstruction early on in a patient’s course – save full pulmonary function testing for later
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

pearl: Don’t be afraid to try empiric therapies! Proton pump inhibitors, antihistamines, intranasal steroids and even inhaled corticosteroids are generally low-risk and will treat many cases of chronic cough
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

evidence_links.rationale: Côté 2020 supports empiric ICS trial with close follow-up.
Kahrilas 2016 cautions against PPI alone without reflux symptoms, tempering empiric PPI use.
Smith 2016 supports PPI trial for suspected reflux-related cough.

pearl: Feel empowered to refer these patients to pulmonary… but also feel empowered to try the aforementioned therapies and start an initial work up
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

evidence_links.rationale: Michaudet 2017 supports empiric therapy approach for undifferentiated cough.

pearl: Dr. Hayward recommends several exam keys that can be helpful in assessing a patient with chronic cough
episode_title: #241 Chronic Cough

evidence_links.rationale: Spicknall 2005 supports clubbing as an exam clue for systemic disease.

pearl: Wilderness medicine is practicing medicine in an austere environment.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: Clinicians from any specialty can get involved in wilderness medicine and pursue additional training.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: Always bring an emergency kit on outdoor expeditions.  Specific contents beyond basic first aid supplies, such as pharmaceuticals, will vary based on the location and nature of the expedition..
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: Many injuries and adverse events in the wilderness start with assuming “best case scenario” or taking shortcuts. Always prepare for the unexpected, including the need to spend the night in the wilderness.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: High altitude sickness is a spectrum of illness that includes acute mountain sickness and that can progress to high altitude cerebral edema; High altitude pulmonary edema is a separate altitude-related clinical entity. AMS and HACE can be prevented by slow ascent or prophylaxis with acetazolamide 125mg BID.  Treatment includes acetazolamide 250mg BID or dexamethasone.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

evidence_links.rationale: Risk stratification for who needs altitude sickness prevention based on AMS history and ascent.
RCT supporting acetazolamide 125mg BID for prevention of altitude sickness/HACE.
Guideline on acetazolamide for prevention and dexamethasone for treatment of AMS/HACE.
Observational survey documenting AMS incidence at altitude.

pearl: The Swiss Model of Hypothermia outlines the severity stages and clinical findings of hypothermia.  Patients with hypothermia need to be rewarmed but also moved very carefully, if at all, to avoid risk of cardiac arrest
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

evidence_links.rationale: The Swiss Model of Hypothermia directly named as basis for severity staging.
Part of Swiss Model, supports careful handling and rewarming to avoid cardiac arrest.
Part of Swiss Model guiding hypothermia management and rewarming.
Supports careful handling and prevention of further heat loss in hypothermia.

pearl: Animal bites need to be treated seriously, with local health organizations vital in determining specific risk of transmissible illnesses such as rabies and post-exposure prophylaxis/treatment needs such as antivenom or antibiotics.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

evidence_links.rationale: Estimates snakebite ED presentations framing bite burden and venomous risk.

pearl: Long feared for the risk of limb loss, tourniquets now have a role in triage in the field and are an essential part of emergency kits.  Advances in local hemostasis such as bandages impregnated with procoagulants are additionally available.
episode_title: #242 Wilderness Medicine: Prepare for Disaster

pearl: SGLT-2 inhibitors lower blood sugar without significant hypoglycemia, encourage weight loss, and lower blood pressure.  They reduce heart failure complications, slow progression of CKD, reduce CVD, and lower death rates.
episode_title: #243 Diabetes Triple Distilled

pearl: GLP-1 agonists lower blood sugar without significant hypoglycemia and encourage weight loss.  They reduce cardiovascular disease outcomes and lower death rates.
episode_title: #243 Diabetes Triple Distilled

evidence_links.rationale: Lee 2020 supports GLP-1 agonists reducing death and CV outcomes.
Zheng 2018 supports GLP-1 agonists reducing death and CV outcomes.
Lee 2020 supports GLP-1 CV and mortality benefits in secondary prevention.

pearl: Consider de-intensifying therapy in diabetic patients treated with pharmacotherapy with an A1c <6.5% if they do not have specific indications for SGLT-2 inhibitors or GLP-1 agonists.

pearl: Hip anatomy is very simple: It’s a ball in a bowl-shaped socket. There’s a little rim of “gristle” around the socket rim (the labrum). There’s also a bump on the lateral side of the femur that can cause pain when the IT band rubs up against it. These are the parts that matter 90% of the time when a patient comes in with hip pain.
episode_title: #149: Hip Pain for Primary Care

pearl: The analgesic effect of buprenorphine only lasts 6-8 hours. Therefore, to treat acute or chronic pain, dose buprenorphine q6-q8h and increase the total daily dose.
